user_query: I have a VCF file with germline exome variants from a patient with a suspected rare Mendelian disorder. I want to find potentially pathogenic coding variants.

pick.note: Use MANE filtering downstream instead for flexibility

per_gene.note: Not needed when keeping all transcripts for detailed review

most_severe.note: Need detailed per-transcript annotations (SIFT, PolyPhen, HGVS)

summary.note: Need detailed per-transcript annotations, not a summary

gnomad_af.note: Filter downstream for AF < 0.01 to remove common variants

protein.note: HGVS notation and gene symbols are sufficient for clinical reporting

regulatory.note: Not needed for coding-focused exome analysis; would slow runtime

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

clinvar_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

gnomad_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

justification: For rare Mendelian disease, focus on coding variants that are rare in the population and predicted to be damaging. MANE Select gives one representative transcript per gene, reducing noise. SIFT, PolyPhen-2, CADD, AlphaMissense, and REVEL provide multiple complementary pathogenicity assessments for missense variants. ClinVar flags variants with existing pathogenicity assertions. gnomAD exome frequencies let you filter out common variants (AF < 0.01) unlikely to cause rare disease. SpliceAI catches potential splice-disrupting variants. HGVS notation is standard for clinical reporting. Gene-phenotype associations help prioritise variants in known disease genes. Regulatory annotations are disabled because exome data primarily captures coding regions. --most_severe and --summary are incompatible with the per-transcript annotations needed here.

user_query: I have a tumour-normal paired VCF with somatic variants from a cancer patient. I need to prioritise potential driver mutations and identify actionable variants.

mane_select.note: Different oncology databases use different transcripts; keep all transcripts

pick.note: Need all transcripts since oncology databases may reference different ones

per_gene.note: Need all transcripts per gene for cross-referencing oncology databases

most_severe.note: Need SIFT/PolyPhen scores and HGVS notation for clinical reporting

summary.note: Need detailed per-transcript annotations for actionability assessment

revel.note: REVEL is optimised for rare disease, less relevant for somatic drivers

gnomad_af.note: Do NOT filter by AF — some cancer drivers like BRAF V600E are present in gnomAD

spliceai.note: Splicing less commonly actionable in cancer analysis

protein.note: Protein IDs useful for linking to structural analysis and drug target databases

regulatory.note: Focus on coding driver mutations for clinical actionability

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

clinvar_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

gnomad_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

justification: Somatic cancer analysis requires keeping all transcripts rather than restricting to MANE, because different oncology databases (COSMIC, OncoKB, CIViC) reference different transcript IDs. The canonical flag helps identify primary transcripts without discarding others. SIFT, PolyPhen, and CADD assess functional impact of missense drivers. Critically, gnomAD frequencies should NOT be used as a filter — well-known cancer drivers like BRAF V600E appear in gnomAD at low frequency. ClinVar and gene-phenotype associations help identify variants with known oncogenic relevance. HGVS notation and gene symbols are essential for matching to clinical knowledge bases. Protein identifiers help link to structural analysis and drug target databases. Domains help identify variants in hotspot functional regions (e.g., kinase domains). --most_severe and --summary would suppress all these detailed annotations.

user_query: I have GWAS-significant SNPs in intergenic and intronic regions. I need to understand their potential regulatory impact and identify candidate target genes.

mane_select.note: Not relevant for non-coding regulatory analysis

per_gene.note: Not useful for non-coding regulatory variants

most_severe.note: Need regulatory annotations which most_severe suppresses

sift.note: SIFT only works on missense coding variants — useless for non-coding

polyphen.note: PolyPhen only works on missense coding variants — useless for non-coding

cadd.note: CADD works on non-coding variants unlike SIFT/PolyPhen

gnomad_af.note: Use genome not exome — non-coding variants are poorly covered by exome

af_1kg.note: Population-specific frequencies useful for GWAS context

regulatory.note: ESSENTIAL — identifies promoters, enhancers, TF binding sites overlapping GWAS hits

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

clinvar_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

gnomad_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

justification: For GWAS follow-up of non-coding variants, regulatory annotations are the most critical option — they identify whether SNPs fall in promoters, enhancers, CTCF binding sites, or transcription factor binding motifs from the Ensembl Regulatory Build. SIFT and PolyPhen are completely useless here as they only score missense coding variants. CADD is the one pathogenicity predictor that works on non-coding variants. gnomAD genome (not exome) frequencies are needed because non-coding variants are poorly captured by exome sequencing. 1000 Genomes frequencies provide useful population stratification data for GWAS context. Gene-phenotype associations help connect regulatory regions to known disease genes. Protein identifiers and domains are not relevant for non-coding variants.

user_query: I want to annotate common variants across multiple populations to compare allele frequencies and functional annotation patterns. My dataset has about 2 million variants from whole genome sequencing.

mane_select.note: Not the focus for population-level analysis

pick.note: Reduces output volume dramatically for 2M variants

most_severe.note: Need at least basic consequence type per transcript; --pick is better

summary.note: --pick provides cleaner output than --summary for downstream analysis

sift.note: Not needed for population-level frequency comparison

polyphen.note: Not needed for population-level frequency comparison

af_1kg.note: Continental population frequencies are the primary interest

hgvs.note: Not needed for population frequency analysis

protein.note: Not needed for population frequency comparison

regulatory.note: Would slow runtime significantly for 2M variants

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

clinvar_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

gnomad_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

justification: Population genetics analysis focuses on frequency distribution across populations rather than individual variant pathogenicity. Enable both gnomAD and 1000 Genomes to get comprehensive population-specific allele frequencies for comparison. Use --pick to get one consequence per variant — with 2 million variants, full transcript output would be enormous and unwieldy. Pathogenicity predictors (SIFT, PolyPhen, CADD) are not needed since the goal is frequency comparison, not clinical prioritisation. Regulatory annotations would significantly slow the analysis for 2M variants. Gene symbols are useful for readability. --check_existing gets rsIDs for cross-referencing.

user_query: I have structural variants (large deletions and duplications) called from whole genome sequencing. I need to identify clinically relevant SVs and filter out common ones.

mane_select.note: SVs typically span multiple genes; single transcript selection is less relevant

pick.note: SVs may affect multiple genes; need to see all

per_gene.note: One consequence per gene is useful for SVs spanning multiple genes

most_severe.note: Need per-gene consequences, not just one overall consequence

summary.note: Need structured per-gene output for clinical review

cadd.note: Not designed for large structural variants

clinvar_sv.note: ClinVar SV clinical significance with overlap matching

gnomad_af.note: Standard gnomAD AF is for SNVs/indels, not SVs

gnomad_sv.note: gnomAD SV frequencies with overlap threshold (e.g., 80%)

hgvs.note: HGVS notation is complex and less standardised for SVs

protein.note: Not meaningful for large structural variants

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

justification: Structural variant analysis requires specialised options introduced in Ensembl release 115. gnomAD SV frequencies (with configurable overlap threshold, e.g., 80%) let you filter common SVs. ClinVar SV annotations flag SVs with existing clinical significance. Standard pathogenicity predictors (SIFT, PolyPhen, CADD, AlphaMissense) are not applicable to SVs — they are designed for SNVs and small indels. --per_gene is useful here because SVs often span multiple genes and you want the most severe consequence for each affected gene. Regulatory annotations are important because SVs may delete or disrupt enhancers, promoters, or other regulatory elements, causing disease through non-coding mechanisms. Gene-phenotype associations help when SVs overlap known disease genes.

user_query: I'm investigating variants near splice sites that might cause aberrant splicing in a rare disease cohort. I have both exonic and intronic variants within 50bp of exon-intron boundaries.

pick.note: Different transcripts have different splice sites; need all

per_gene.note: Need all transcripts to see all splice sites

most_severe.note: Incompatible with SpliceAI, SIFT, PolyPhen scores

sift.note: Some exonic variants may also be missense

polyphen.note: Some exonic variants may also be missense

alphamissense.note: Focus is on splicing, not missense pathogenicity

spliceai.note: CRITICAL — provides quantitative splice disruption scores

maxentscan.note: Complementary splice site scoring using maximum entropy models

protein.note: Splicing analysis focuses on transcript-level effects, not protein IDs

clinvar_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

gnomad_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

justification: Splice variant analysis requires specialised splicing prediction tools. SpliceAI is the most important option here — it provides quantitative delta scores for four splice event types (acceptor gain/loss, donor gain/loss) with scores >= 0.5 being high confidence. MaxEntScan complements SpliceAI with maximum entropy-based splice site scoring that compares reference vs alternate allele scores. VEP natively predicts splice_donor_variant and splice_acceptor_variant consequences for canonical ±1-2 positions, but these plugins extend prediction to deeper intronic positions and exonic splice-affecting variants. SIFT and PolyPhen are kept because some near-splice exonic variants may also be missense. CADD integrates splicing signals in its model. MANE Select is useful for clinical reporting of splice variants. --most_severe and --summary would suppress these critical per-transcript splice scores.

user_query: I just want to quickly look up what rs429358 does. What variant is it and is it clinically relevant?

pick.note: Single variant lookup — one consequence is sufficient

per_gene.note: Using --pick instead; single variant unlikely to span multiple genes

most_severe.note: Want SIFT/PolyPhen scores which most_severe suppresses

summary.note: Want detailed annotation, not just consequence types

protein.note: Overkill for a quick lookup; HGVS is sufficient

regulatory.note: Overkill for a single variant lookup

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

clinvar_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

gnomad_sv.note: Only relevant for structural variant analysis

justification: For a quick single-variant lookup, defaults plus basic pathogenicity annotation is appropriate. Use --pick to get a single clean result rather than multiple transcript annotations. SIFT, PolyPhen, CADD, and AlphaMissense give a quick pathogenicity assessment. ClinVar shows if it has known clinical significance. gnomAD shows population frequency. HGVS and gene symbol make the result immediately readable. No need for regulatory annotations, splicing predictors, or extensive frequency data for a single rsID lookup.

user_query: I have variants from a CRISPR gene editing experiment in mouse (Mus musculus, GRCm39). I need to annotate the functional consequences of the introduced variants.

transcript_set.note: RefSeq coverage is patchy for mouse; use Ensembl

mane_select.note: MANE is human-only — not available for mouse

canonical.note: Use canonical since MANE is unavailable

pick.note: One consequence per variant is typically sufficient for editing experiments

most_severe.note: Need SIFT scores and transcript details for CRISPR validation

summary.note: Need detailed annotations for each editing site

protein.note: Not essential for CRISPR validation; domains covers functional regions

gene_phenotype.note: Mouse phenotype data available from MGI

regulatory.note: Limited regulatory build data for mouse

maxentscan.note: Not needed unless specifically investigating splice-site variants

justification: Non-human species analysis requires careful attention to which options are species-restricted. For mouse, many human-specific resources are unavailable: MANE Select (human-only), PolyPhen-2 (human-only), CADD (human-only), AlphaMissense (human-only), REVEL (human-only), ClinVar (human-only), gnomAD (human-only), and 1000 Genomes (human-only). SIFT is one of the few pathogenicity predictors that works across species. Use Ensembl/GENCODE transcripts as RefSeq coverage for mouse is less comprehensive. The canonical flag replaces MANE for identifying primary transcripts. Protein domains (InterPro) work across species and are valuable for assessing CRISPR edit consequences. Gene-phenotype data from MGI (Mouse Genome Informatics) is available through Ensembl. --most_severe and --summary would suppress the SIFT scores and transcript details needed for CRISPR validation.
